title: Boundary Protection
controlText: Monitor, control, and protect organizational communications (i.e., information transmitted or received by organizational information systems) at the external boundaries and key internal boundaries of the information systems.
plainEnglish: Define and defend the perimeter — both the external boundary between your network and the internet, and the key internal boundaries that separate one segment from another (e.g., development from production, CUI enclave from corporate). 'Monitor' means logging and alerting; 'control' means deciding what gets through; 'protect' means encryption or other safeguards on data crossing those boundaries. This is the foundational SC practice that several others extend.

objectives.text: the external system boundary is defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented external boundary — typically firewalls, gateways, cloud service edges, internet egress points. Boundary diagram or network architecture document shows where the org's network ends and external networks begin.
commonEvidence: Network architecture diagram with external boundary marked
System security plan section on system boundary

objectives.text: key internal system boundaries are defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented internal boundaries between segments — CUI enclave vs corporate, dev vs prod, OT vs IT, DMZ vs internal. Internal firewalls, VLANs, or segmentation gateways enforce these boundaries.

objectives.text: communications are monitored at the external system boundary;
objectives.whatToLookFor: External boundary traffic is logged and reviewed — firewall logs forwarded to SIEM, IDS/IPS deployed, alerts on suspicious patterns. Logs are actually reviewed, not just collected.

objectives.text: communications are monitored at key internal boundaries;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Internal boundary traffic is monitored — particularly traffic crossing into or out of the CUI enclave. East-west monitoring, not just north-south.
commonEvidence: Network detection tools spanning internal segments
Sample alerts from internal boundary monitoring

objectives.text: communications are controlled at the external system boundary;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Default-deny firewall posture at the external boundary, with explicit allow rules for required traffic only. Documented rule purpose and ownership. Periodic firewall rule reviews.
commonEvidence: Firewall rule sets with documented purpose
Change management evidence for rule modifications

objectives.text: communications are controlled at key internal boundaries;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Internal segmentation enforced via firewall rules or NAC. CUI enclave restricts inbound and outbound traffic to documented sources/destinations. No flat networks where any segment can talk to any other.

objectives.text: communications are protected at the external system boundary; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Encryption applied to external traffic carrying sensitive data — TLS for web, VPN tunnels for remote access, IPsec for site-to-site. Plain HTTP, telnet, FTP not used to transmit sensitive data across the boundary.
commonEvidence: Disabled plaintext protocols at the boundary

objectives.text: communications are protected at key internal boundaries.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Encryption applied to internal traffic carrying sensitive data, particularly into and out of the CUI enclave. Internal TLS, encrypted east-west traffic where applicable.
commonEvidence: Service mesh or zero-trust internal encryption

commonGaps: Flat network with no internal segmentation
Boundary diagram does not match actual network topology
Firewall rules accumulate over years with no documented purpose or owner
Internal traffic not monitored — only north-south at the perimeter
Allow-any rules from internal segments to the internet
CUI enclave defined on paper but not actually segmented at the network layer
IDS/IPS deployed in passive mode but no alerts are reviewed

title: Public-Access System Separation
controlText: Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are physically or logically separated from internal networks.
plainEnglish: Public-facing systems — corporate website, customer portals, public APIs — must live on a separate subnet (a DMZ) that is isolated from internal networks. Compromising a public web server should not give an attacker a path to internal CUI systems. Cloud equivalents apply: a public-facing cloud environment must be architecturally separated from environments containing CUI.

objectives.text: publicly accessible system components are identified; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented inventory of all publicly-accessible components — internet-facing web servers, customer portals, APIs, VPN gateways, public DNS. Includes cloud services that face the public internet.

objectives.text: subnetworks for publicly accessible system components are physically or logically separated from internal networks.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Public components reside in a DMZ subnet (on-prem) or isolated cloud account/VNet (cloud). Default-deny rules block DMZ-to-internal traffic except for specifically authorized flows. No internal systems sharing a subnet with public-facing components.
commonEvidence: Firewall rules between DMZ and internal networks

commonGaps: Public web server in the same subnet as internal application servers
DMZ exists but has unrestricted access to internal databases
Cloud public-facing resources in the same VPC as CUI workloads
No external attack surface scanning to verify what's actually exposed
Public APIs running on internal-facing infrastructure with port forwarding

title: Security Engineering
controlText: Employ architectural designs, software development techniques, and systems engineering principles that promote effective information security within organizational systems.
plainEnglish: Security has to be designed into systems from the start — not bolted on after the fact. This means having documented security architecture principles (defense in depth, least privilege, secure-by-default), applying them when designing new systems or major upgrades, and training developers on secure development. Often demonstrated through reference to NIST SP 800-160. Legacy systems get treated to the extent feasible.

objectives.text: architectural designs that promote effective information security are identified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented security architecture principles or reference architectures — defense in depth, segmentation, zero trust, secure-by-default. May reference NIST SP 800-160 or industry frameworks.

objectives.text: software development techniques that promote effective information security are identified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented secure software development techniques — input validation, output encoding, parameterized queries, dependency scanning, code review requirements. Often part of an SSDLC document.

objectives.text: systems engineering principles that promote effective information security are identified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented systems engineering principles — threat modeling, security requirements in design, security testing, risk-based decision making during design.
commonEvidence: Security requirements template for new systems

objectives.text: identified architectural designs that promote effective information security are employed;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence the principles are actually applied — recent system designs reference the architecture document, design reviews check against principles, deviations are documented.
commonEvidence: Recent design documents referencing architecture principles

objectives.text: identified software development techniques that promote effective information security are employed; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: SSDLC techniques in active use — code reviews happening, SAST/DAST in CI/CD, dependency scanning, secure coding training records.

objectives.text: identified systems engineering principles that promote effective information security are employed.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Engineering principles in use during real projects — threat models exist for in-scope systems, security requirements traceability, risk decisions documented.
commonEvidence: Risk acceptance records for design decisions

commonGaps: Security architecture document exists but is years out of date
SSDLC defined but not enforced — projects ship without security review
Threat modeling done once for major systems, never updated
No security requirements gathering during design phase
Legacy systems have never been brought up to current architecture standards with no plan to address

title: Role Separation
controlText: Separate user functionality from system management functionality.
plainEnglish: User-facing functions and system administration functions must be separated — physically (different machines), logically (different accounts and interfaces), or via virtualization (jump hosts, separate VLANs). General users should not see admin interfaces; admins should not perform admin work from their general-use accounts or workstations. This complements AC.L2-3.1.6 but focuses on the system-level separation rather than account usage.

objectives.text: user functionality is identified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented identification of user-facing functions — applications, services, interfaces that general users interact with as part of their job.

objectives.text: system management functionality is identified; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented identification of system management functions — admin consoles, configuration interfaces, management plane access. Separated from user functions in the documentation.

objectives.text: user functionality is separated from system management functionality.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical separation enforced — admin interfaces on different URLs/networks, jump hosts for admin access, separate VLANs for management traffic, different authentication for admin functions. Verified through architecture review and testing.
commonEvidence: Network segmentation diagram showing management VLAN
Separate admin and user URL/portal documentation

commonArtifacts: User vs system management function inventory
Network architecture showing management plane separation
Jump host or PAW deployment documentation
commonGaps: Admin interfaces accessible from the same network as user workstations with no segmentation
Cloud admin consoles accessed from general-purpose workstations
No jump host or PAW — admins access systems directly from daily-use machines
Application admin functions exposed in the same UI as user functions, gated only by RBAC
Management traffic and user traffic share the same VLAN

title: Shared Resource Control
controlText: Prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources.
plainEnglish: Shared system resources — memory, CPU caches, hard disks, registers — must not leak information from one user or process to another. This is typically handled by the operating system (memory zeroing, process isolation) and chip-level mitigations (Meltdown/Spectre patches). The assessment is largely about confirming that systems are patched and configured to prevent shared-resource leakage, not building anything new.

objectives.text: unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources is prevented.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Operating systems patched against known shared-resource vulnerabilities (Meltdown, Spectre, MDS, Foreshadow, etc.). Process isolation enforced. File system permissions prevent users from reading each other's data. Cloud environments use providers with documented tenant isolation.
commonEvidence: OS hardening baseline addressing process isolation

commonArtifacts: Cloud tenant isolation documentation from provider
commonGaps: Unpatched systems vulnerable to known shared-resource attacks (Meltdown, Spectre family)
Multi-user systems where users can read each other's home directories
Shared cloud tenancy with no documented isolation guarantees from the provider
Custom applications that don't clear sensitive data from memory after use

title: Network Communication by Exception
controlText: Deny network communications traffic by default and allow network communications traffic by exception (i.e., deny all, permit by exception).
plainEnglish: Firewall and routing rules must default to deny — all traffic is blocked unless an explicit rule allows it. This applies in both directions (inbound AND outbound) and at all enforcement points (perimeter, internal segmentation, host-based firewalls). This practice extends SC.L1-3.13.1 by adding the specific deny-all-permit-by-exception requirement. Allow-any-outbound is a frequent finding.

objectives.text: network communications traffic is denied by default; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Firewall rule sets configured with implicit-deny at the end of every rule chain. Outbound rules default to deny, not just inbound. Verified through configuration review of perimeter firewalls, internal segmentation firewalls, and host-based firewalls.

objectives.text: network communications traffic is allowed by exception.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Each allow rule has documented business justification, owner, and review date. Periodic firewall rule reviews remove stale rules. No 'temporary' allow-any rules left in place.
commonEvidence: Change management evidence for rule additions

commonArtifacts: Firewall rule sets with documented purpose per rule
commonGaps: Allow-any outbound rule from internal networks to the internet
Host-based firewalls disabled across the environment
Cloud security groups with 0.0.0.0/0 inbound rules
Firewall rules accumulated over years, many no longer needed but not removed
No documented justification for individual allow rules

title: Split Tunneling
controlText: Prevent remote devices from simultaneously establishing non-remote connections with organizational systems and communicating via some other connection to resources in external networks (i.e., split tunneling).
plainEnglish: When a remote device is connected to the corporate network via VPN, all of its network traffic must flow through that VPN — not just the corporate-bound traffic. Split tunneling, where a device sends some traffic over the VPN and some directly to the internet, creates a path where a compromised device could be used as a bridge between an attacker on the internet and the internal network. Most VPN clients have a setting to enforce full tunneling.

objectives.text: remote devices are prevented from simultaneously establishing non-remote connections with the system and communicating via some other connection to resources in external networks (i.e., split tunneling).
objectives.whatToLookFor: VPN clients configured to enforce full tunneling — all traffic routed through the VPN, no direct-to-internet path while VPN is active. Verified through VPN client configuration and endpoint testing. Mobile devices configured with always-on VPN where applicable.
commonEvidence: VPN client configuration showing full tunnel mode
Group Policy or MDM enforcing full tunneling
Endpoint test confirming all traffic routes through VPN
VPN gateway logs showing client-side tunneling enforcement

commonGaps: VPN configured with split tunneling enabled 'for performance'
Users can disable the VPN client at will, bypassing tunneling enforcement
Mobile devices have VPN but it doesn't auto-connect — users frequently work without it
BYOD devices with VPN access where the org has no enforcement of client config

title: Data in Transit
controlText: Implement cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure of CUI during transmission unless otherwise protected by alternative physical safeguards.
plainEnglish: CUI in transit over any network — internal or external — must be encrypted using FIPS-validated cryptography, unless the physical transmission medium is itself protected (e.g., a Protected Distribution System). Most environments meet this through TLS (modern versions only), VPN, or encrypted secure file transfer. The FIPS-validation requirement is identical to AC.L2-3.1.13 and the same evidence usually applies. Internal transmission is in scope, not just internet-bound.

objectives.text: cryptographic mechanisms intended to prevent unauthorized disclosure of CUI are identified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented cryptographic mechanisms used to protect CUI in transit — TLS for web traffic, IPsec for site-to-site, SFTP for file transfer, encrypted email gateways. Each tied to a FIPS-validated module on the CMVP list.

objectives.text: alternative physical safeguards intended to prevent unauthorized disclosure of CUI are identified; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Where cryptography is not used, documented physical safeguards — typically a Protected Distribution System (PDS) with physical tamper protection. For most commercial organizations this is N/A and the answer is 'we use cryptography everywhere.'
commonEvidence: Documented determination that PDS is not used
Physical security controls for transmission infrastructure

objectives.text: either cryptographic mechanisms or alternative physical safeguards are implemented to prevent unauthorized disclosure of CUI during transmission.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Active enforcement that CUI is encrypted in transit — TLS-only on web services, encrypted file transfer required, no plaintext protocols for CUI. Spot-check by attempting plaintext transmission and confirming it's blocked or alerted.
commonEvidence: TLS scan results showing FIPS-only configurations
DLP rules detecting plaintext CUI transmission
Approved secure file transfer tool deployment evidence

commonArtifacts: Cryptographic mechanism inventory with CMVP references
commonGaps: CUI sent via standard email without encryption gateway
Internal HTTP traffic carrying CUI between application servers
FTP or SCP without modern cipher requirements still in use
TLS configuration allows non-FIPS cipher suites as fallback
No DLP detection for plaintext CUI in transit — assumed compliance

title: Connections Termination
controlText: Terminate network connections associated with communications sessions at the end of the sessions or after a defined period of inactivity.
plainEnglish: Network connections must terminate cleanly — both at the natural end of a session and after a defined period of inactivity. This applies to internal and external networks. Different from session termination at the user level (AC.L2-3.1.11): this addresses the underlying network connection itself, including TCP timeouts, VPN session limits, and firewall idle timeouts. Prevents resource exhaustion and stale connection abuse.

objectives.text: a period of inactivity to terminate network connections associated with communications sessions is defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented inactivity timeout for network connections — typically embedded in firewall, VPN, and load balancer configurations. Defined per protocol where appropriate.
commonEvidence: VPN and load balancer timeout settings

objectives.text: network connections associated with communications sessions are terminated at the end of the sessions; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Network connections close cleanly when sessions end — TCP FIN handshake completed, VPN tunnels torn down on logout, no lingering half-open connections.

objectives.text: network connections associated with communications sessions are terminated after the defined period of inactivity.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Idle timeouts actually fire — verified through firewall connection logs, VPN session reports, load balancer telemetry. Stale connections don't accumulate.
commonEvidence: Firewall connection logs showing idle disconnects

commonGaps: VPN sessions persist for days due to extremely long timeouts
Load balancer keepalives prevent idle disconnect
Custom applications hold network connections open indefinitely
Stale TCP connections accumulate on stateful devices, eventually causing resource exhaustion

title: Key Management
controlText: Establish and manage cryptographic keys for cryptography employed in organizational systems.
plainEnglish: Wherever you use cryptography, you have keys — and those keys must themselves be managed: generated securely, distributed safely, stored protected, used appropriately, rotated periodically, revoked when compromised, and destroyed when retired. Standard practice is to use a Key Management System (KMS) or hardware security module (HSM) rather than ad-hoc handling. NIST SP 800-57 is the authoritative reference. This complements every cryptography practice in the framework.

objectives.text: cryptographic keys are established whenever cryptography is employed; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented key generation procedures — keys generated with appropriate strength using validated random number generators. Asymmetric keys generated in HSM or hardware-backed keystore where possible.

objectives.text: cryptographic keys are managed whenever cryptography is employed.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented key management lifecycle — distribution, storage, use, backup, recovery, rotation, revocation, destruction. Access to key material restricted to specifically authorized personnel. Key escrow or recovery procedures documented.
commonEvidence: KMS access control configuration and audit logs
Key rotation evidence (BitLocker recovery keys, certificate renewals, API key rotation)

commonArtifacts: KMS or HSM configuration and access logs
commonGaps: BitLocker recovery keys stored in a spreadsheet on a network share
API keys never rotated, even after the original developer left the company
TLS certificates expire because nobody is tracking renewal dates
No documented key destruction procedure for retired keys
Service account passwords (a form of key) shared widely with no rotation when staff change
PKI infrastructure but no documented certificate authority compromise procedure

title: CUI Encryption
controlText: Employ FIPS-validated cryptography when used to protect the confidentiality of CUI.
plainEnglish: When cryptography is used to protect CUI, the cryptographic module must be FIPS 140-2 (or 140-3) validated — listed on the NIST Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP) website. This is the central practice that several others reference: AC.L2-3.1.13 (remote access), AC.L2-3.1.19 (mobile), MP.L2-3.8.6 (portable storage), SC.L2-3.13.8 (data in transit), and SC.L2-3.13.16 (data at rest) all rely on this practice for their FIPS requirements. Using AES-256 from a non-validated library does NOT meet this standard.

objectives.text: FIPS-validated cryptography is employed to protect the confidentiality of CUI.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Inventory of cryptographic modules in use to protect CUI, each tied to a CMVP certificate number on the NIST website. Modules operated in their FIPS-approved mode where applicable. Spot-check by looking up a few certificates to confirm they're current and the module version matches what's deployed.
commonEvidence: FIPS-validated cryptographic module inventory with CMVP certificate numbers
Configuration evidence showing FIPS mode enabled (BitLocker, OpenSSL, OS-level FIPS mode)
Verification records cross-checking deployed versions against CMVP listings

commonArtifacts: FIPS mode enablement records (Windows, RHEL, application-level)
commonGaps: Open-source cryptographic libraries used for CUI without an associated CMVP certificate
FIPS mode available but not enabled on Windows or Linux endpoints handling CUI
Self-signed crypto implementations in custom applications
CMVP certificate exists but for a different module version than what's actually deployed
Cloud KMS use without confirming the CMVP-validated configuration
Confusion between 'FIPS-approved algorithm' (e.g., AES) and 'FIPS-validated module' — the latter is required

title: Collaborative Device Control
controlText: Prohibit remote activation of collaborative computing devices and provide indication of devices in use to users present at the device.
plainEnglish: Devices like webcams, microphones, networked whiteboards, and conference room equipment must (1) not be remotely activatable by an attacker, and (2) provide a clear visual or audible signal when they're in use. This addresses the risk of covert surveillance through compromised collaboration equipment. Dedicated video conferencing systems where one party initiates the call (Zoom, Teams) are typically excluded.

objectives.text: collaborative computing devices are identified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Inventory of collaborative computing devices in scope — webcams, microphones, conference room equipment, networked whiteboards, smart speakers if present. Excludes user-initiated video conferencing where the user must accept the call.

objectives.text: collaborative computing devices provide indication to users of devices in use; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Devices have indicator lights (camera in-use LED, microphone activity light) or other unambiguous signals. For devices without built-in indicators, manual mechanisms exist — door signage, conference room booking systems showing in-use state.
commonEvidence: Conference room signage or booking display photos

objectives.text: remote activation of collaborative computing devices is prohibited.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Devices configured to prevent remote activation without local user consent — webcam software requires user prompt, conference room systems require local activation, group policies disable remote camera/microphone access where applicable.
commonEvidence: Group Policy or MDM settings restricting camera/microphone access

commonGaps: Smart conference room equipment that can be remotely activated by anyone with credentials
Webcams without functional in-use indicator LEDs
Networked microphones in conference rooms with no notification mechanism
Smart speakers in offices that handle CUI conversations
BYOD policy that doesn't address camera/microphone use during work calls

title: Mobile Code
controlText: Control and monitor the use of mobile code.
plainEnglish: Mobile code — software downloaded and executed on the user's machine without explicit installation, like JavaScript, ActiveX, Java applets, Flash, PDF scripts, or VBScript macros — must be controlled. Define which types are allowed under what conditions, monitor their use, and block or restrict types known to be high-risk. Modern environments typically address this via browser security policies, application allow-listing, and macro restrictions.

objectives.text: use of mobile code is controlled; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented policy on mobile code — which types are allowed, restricted, or blocked. Technical enforcement via browser policies (disabling ActiveX, restricting JavaScript on certain sites), application configuration (Office macro restrictions), and endpoint controls.
commonEvidence: Mobile code policy with defined types and restrictions

objectives.text: use of mobile code is monitored.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Detection capability for mobile code use — EDR alerts on suspicious script execution, web proxy logs of mobile code activity, alerts on macro-enabled documents from external sources.
commonEvidence: EDR/SIEM alerts for mobile code execution
Web proxy logs and review records

commonArtifacts: EDR/SIEM rules for mobile code monitoring
commonGaps: No defined mobile code policy — defaults to whatever browsers and Office allow
Office macros from external sources allowed without prompting
Legacy applications still requiring ActiveX or Java applets without restriction
No monitoring or alerting on script execution patterns
User can override browser security policies

title: Voice over Internet Protocol
controlText: Control and monitor the use of Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technologies.
plainEnglish: VoIP systems — Microsoft Teams Phone, Cisco Webex Calling, RingCentral, on-prem PBX systems — must be controlled and monitored. This means defined approved VoIP solutions, configuration that restricts use to authorized users, and monitoring for unauthorized VoIP applications (e.g., personal Skype/Discord/etc. used for work). VoIP traffic carrying sensitive conversations should be encrypted.

objectives.text: use of Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technologies is controlled; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented approved VoIP technologies. Configuration restricts use — encrypted call signaling/media (SRTP), authentication required, admin functions protected. Unauthorized VoIP applications blocked or detected.
commonEvidence: Web filtering or DLP rules blocking unauthorized VoIP

objectives.text: use of Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technologies is monitored.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Active monitoring of VoIP — call logs, configuration change logs, alerts on unusual patterns, detection of unauthorized VoIP applications. Logs forwarded to SIEM.
commonEvidence: Detection rules for unauthorized VoIP applications
VoIP system patching and update records

commonArtifacts: Web filtering rules for unauthorized VoIP
commonGaps: VoIP system uses unencrypted SIP/RTP for signaling and media
No monitoring of VoIP system administrative actions
Personal VoIP applications (Discord, Skype, WhatsApp) used for work calls without restriction
VoIP system not patched on the regular software patching cycle
Voice mailboxes have weak or default passwords

title: Communications Authenticity
controlText: Protect the authenticity of communications sessions.
plainEnglish: Communications sessions must be protected against man-in-the-middle attacks, session hijacking, and false-information injection — both ends of every session need confidence that the other end is who it claims to be throughout the session, not just at handshake. Typically achieved through TLS with proper certificate validation, mutual authentication where applicable, and session token protection. Different from authentication of the user (IA family) — this addresses session-level integrity.

objectives.text: the authenticity of communications sessions is protected.
objectives.whatToLookFor: TLS configured correctly across all sessions — strong cipher suites, certificate validation enforced, no acceptance of self-signed or expired certificates without explicit user action. Session tokens generated with sufficient entropy and bound to the authenticated user. Mutual TLS where partner connections require it.
commonEvidence: TLS configuration showing strong cipher suites and certificate validation
Session token generation and binding documentation
Mutual TLS configuration for partner connections
TLS scan results from external testing

commonArtifacts: TLS scan results (Qualys SSL Labs, sslyze)
Mutual TLS configuration for partner connections
commonGaps: TLS configured but accepts certificates with hostname mismatches
Custom applications that don't validate server certificates
Session tokens predictable or guessable
Sessions can be hijacked via cross-site scripting because cookies aren't HttpOnly/Secure
Partner integrations rely on unauthenticated webhooks or simple shared secrets

title: Data at Rest
controlText: Protect the confidentiality of CUI at rest.
plainEnglish: CUI sitting on storage — hard drives, databases, file shares, backups, cloud storage buckets — must be protected. Encryption is the most common approach (and when used, the cryptography must be FIPS-validated per SC.L2-3.13.11), but other approaches like physical security (locked closets) or secure offline storage are acceptable. The intent is confidentiality of the data, not a specific technology. Backup data is in scope and frequently overlooked.

objectives.text: the confidentiality of CUI at rest is protected.
objectives.whatToLookFor: CUI storage locations identified, with documented protection mechanism per location — full-disk encryption, file/folder encryption, database TDE, cloud storage encryption with customer-managed keys, or physical security where encryption is impractical. Backups also protected.
commonEvidence: Disk encryption configuration on servers and workstations
Physical security controls for unencrypted CUI storage

commonArtifacts: Physical security controls for storage areas
commonGaps: Backups stored unencrypted on tapes or cloud
Database encryption enabled but uses provider-managed keys with no customer control
File shares containing CUI without share-level or file-level encryption
Cloud storage buckets with default encryption that doesn't use FIPS-validated modules
Old hard drives retired without secure erasure or destruction
Encryption enabled on production but not on dev/test environments that contain CUI copies
